aliases: Baked Camembert with Honey and Walnuts

english: Baked Camembert with Honey and Walnuts

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a Camembert cheese entry; baked preparation with honey, walnuts, and crackers is not separately coded. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers Camembert cheese as a base ingredient. The baked dish with accompaniments (honey, walnuts, crackers) is not separately coded in FoodEx2; base cheese code used as closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for baked Camembert dish. Not fabricated.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of baked Camembert is approximately 250g, comprising a 125g wheel of baked Camembert cheese accompanied by a drizzle of honey, a small handful of walnuts, and a portion of crackers.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Camembert Cheese (FDC ID 173414) combined with component estimates for honey, walnuts, and crackers
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values for the composite dish are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for Camembert cheese (FDC ID 173414), honey (FDC ID 169640), walnuts (FDC ID 170187), and plain crackers (FDC ID 172023). The input record's per_100g values (energy 300 kcal, protein 10g, fat 24g, carbohydrate 10g, sugar 6g, sodium 320mg, dietary fibre 1.2g, calcium 160mg, iron 0.6mg, cholesterol 48mg, saturated fat 14g, trans fat 0.6g) are broadly consistent with a Camembert-dominant composite dish and are retained with medium confidence. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and micronutrient values not reported in the input are set to -1 where not derivable from the approved source for this composite.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI value exists for baked Camembert as a composite dish. GI is estimated at approximately 40 (low) based on the dominant Camembert cheese component (cheese GI ~0–30, negligible carbohydrate contribution) blended with the honey and cracker components. Honey GI ~55–58 (medium) and plain crackers GI ~65–70 (medium-high) per University of Sydney GI Database. The composite dish GI is estimated at ~40 (low-medium boundary) reflecting the cheese-dominant weight (125g of 250g total) diluting the higher-GI accompaniments. Glycemic load per 250g serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (40 × 25) / 100 = 10.0.

hypertension.key_factor: High saturated fat (14g/100g) and borderline sodium (320mg/100g) with low dietary fibre (1.2g/100g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid baked Camembert due to its high saturated fat content (14g/100g), which exceeds the AHA threshold of 5g/100g and contributes to arterial stiffness; sodium at 320mg/100g is borderline elevated, and the honey drizzle adds empty-calorie sugar with minimal fibre offset — if consumed, restrict to a very small portion, omit honey, and choose wholegrain crackers.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Honey-derived added sugar, high-GI crackers, and low dietary fibre (1.2g/100g) elevating glycaemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should exercise caution with baked Camembert as served: the honey topping adds free sugars and the refined crackers carry a high GI (~65–70), creating a moderate glycaemic load per serving; omitting honey, replacing crackers with high-fibre wholegrain alternatives, and retaining walnuts (which improve insulin sensitivity) will substantially reduce the glycaemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 14g/100g and trans fat 0.6g/100g significantly exceed cardiovascular risk thresholds
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Baked Camembert is high-risk for cardiovascular disease patients due to saturated fat at 14g/100g (well above the 5g/100g threshold) and trans fat at 0.6g/100g, both of which raise LDL cholesterol and increase atherosclerotic risk; cholesterol at 48mg/100g adds further burden, and this dish should be consumed only occasionally and in very small portions by individuals with CVD.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat 14g/100g from dairy far exceeds the 5g/100g RA threshold, promoting pro-inflammatory cytokines
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Saturated fat at 14g/100g from Camembert cheese triggers TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory cytokines and is not offset by the walnut omega-3 garnish; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit Camembert to very small portions, omit honey, and consider lower-fat cheese alternatives.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Baked Camembert is a warm cheese dish originating from the Normandy region of France, made by oven-baking a whole 125g wheel of Camembert cheese until the interior becomes soft, molten, and gooey while the rind holds its shape. The dish is typically served topped with a drizzle of honey for sweetness, accompanied by crunchy walnuts and crisp crackers for textural contrast. The flavour profile is rich, creamy, and savoury with earthy mushroom notes from the rind, balanced by the floral sweetness of honey and the bitter crunch of walnuts. Nutritionally, the dish is calorie-dense at approximately 300 kcal per 100g, dominated by fat (24g/100g) of which saturated fat is very high at 14g/100g, making it a significant concern for cardiovascular disease and hypertension. Protein is moderate at 10g/100g. Carbohydrate at 10g/100g is primarily contributed by the honey and crackers, with sugar at 6g/100g. Sodium at 320mg/100g is borderline elevated. Dietary fibre is low at 1.2g/100g. Calcium is notable at 160mg/100g from the cheese. The dish is vegetarian but not vegan, contains milk, gluten, and tree nut allergens. It is popular as a sharing starter or appetiser in European-style restaurants and cafes in Singapore and across Southeast Asia. Clinically, it is unsuitable for individuals with cardiovascular disease, hypertension, or rheumatoid arthritis due to its high saturated fat and trans fat content, and requires caution for type 2 diabetes patients due to honey and cracker-derived glycaemic load.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for Camembert cheese and component ingredients. The input record values are broadly consistent with approved-source data for a Camembert-dominant composite dish and are retained. GI is an analogous estimate as no direct GI value exists for this composite dish. Cultural metadata in the input (primary_cuisine listed as Japanese, ethnic_tags including Japanese Rice Bowl and Alcoholic Drink) was clearly erroneous and has been corrected to French/Normandy. Confidence is medium due to composite dish estimation and absence of a direct approved-source entry for this exact preparation.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite baked Camembert dish (with honey, walnuts, crackers) is not directly available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database; component-level data exists but composite MUFA/PUFA values were not derivable with sufficient confidence for this record. 2. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient and moisture values were not reported in the input record and are not derivable with confidence for this composite dish from the approved sources consulted (USDA FoodData Central). 3. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: Camembert cheese and the accompaniments in this dish contain negligible vitamin C; confirmed as effectively zero per USDA FoodData Central Camembert entry. 4. Input cultural metadata was erroneous (primary_cuisine: Japanese; ethnic_tags: Japanese, Alcoholic Drink, Japanese Rice Bowl; country_of_origin: Singapore) — these have been corrected to reflect the true French/Normandy origin of Camembert. 5. GI is an analogous estimate (~40, low) based on the cheese-dominant composition diluting honey and cracker GI contributions; no direct GI entry exists for baked Camembert as a composite dish in the University of Sydney GI Database. 6. trans_fat_g is 0.6 as reported in the input; this is plausible for a dairy-based cheese product (naturally occurring ruminant trans fats) and is retained from the input source.
